section.title: Misery, Then Hope
section.sectionRationale: The book ends where honesty must begin: in the wreckage of a society where no one upright is left and even the family turns on itself. Yet from that darkness rises one of the great confessions of faith, but as for me, I will look to the LORD. The chapter climbs to a soaring finish, who is a God like You, who pardons iniquity and casts our sins into the depths of the sea, keeping the covenant sworn to Abraham. The prophet's own name, which means who is like the LORD, becomes the last note of the book.
section.thesis: When society and even the household have collapsed, the believer's hope is not in people but in the God of salvation who hears; and the God who is beyond compare pardons iniquity, tramples it underfoot, and casts every sin into the sea, keeping the covenant love He swore to Abraham, fulfilled in Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Israel's great misery, where the godly have vanished and a person's enemies are his own household (1-6); the confession and comfort of the one who waits for the God of salvation in the dark (7-13); and the closing prayer and doxology, who is a God like You who pardons iniquity and casts our sins into the sea, keeping faith to Abraham (14-20).

verses.text: Woe is me! For I am like one gathering summer fruit at the gleaning of the vineyard; there is no cluster to eat, no early fig that I crave.
The godly man has perished from the earth; there is no one upright among men. They all lie in wait for blood; they hunt one another with a net.
Both hands are skilled at evil; the prince and the judge demand a bribe. When the powerful utters his evil desire, they all conspire together.
The best of them is like a brier; the most upright is sharper than a hedge of thorns. The day for your watchmen has come, the day of your visitation. Now is the time of their confusion.
Do not rely on a friend; do not trust in a companion. Seal the doors of your mouth from her who lies in your arms.
For a son dishonors his father, a daughter rises against her mother, and a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law. A man’s enemies are the members of his own household.

units.label: I will wait for the God of my salvation
units.note: though I fall, I will rise

verses.text: But as for me, I will look to the LORD; I will wait for the God of my salvation. My God will hear me.
Do not gloat over me, my enemy! Though I have fallen, I will arise; though I sit in darkness, the LORD will be my light.
Because I have sinned against Him, I must endure the rage of the LORD, until He argues my case and executes justice for me. He will bring me into the light; I will see His righteousness.
Then my enemy will see and will be covered with shame, she who said to me, “Where is the LORD your God?” My eyes will see her; at that time she will be trampled like mud in the streets.
The day for rebuilding your walls will come, the day for extending your boundary.
On that day they will come to you from Assyria and the cities of Egypt, even from Egypt to the Euphrates, from sea to sea and mountain to mountain.
Then the earth will become desolate because of its inhabitants, as the fruit of their deeds.

units.label: Who is a God like You
units.note: He casts our sins into the sea

verses.text: Shepherd with Your staff Your people, the flock of Your inheritance. They live alone in a woodland, surrounded by pastures. Let them graze in Bashan and Gilead, as in the days of old.
As in the days when you came out of Egypt, I will show My wonders.
Nations will see and be ashamed, deprived of all their might. They will put their hands over their mouths, and their ears will become deaf.
They will lick the dust like a snake, like reptiles slithering on the ground. They will come trembling from their strongholds in the presence of the LORD our God; they will tremble in fear of You.

{{mikamokha|Who is a God like You}}, who {{nasa|pardons iniquity}} and passes over the transgression of the remnant of His inheritance, who does not retain His anger forever, because He delights in {{chesed|loving devotion}}?
He will again have compassion on us; He will vanquish our iniquities. You will cast out all our sins into the depths of the sea.
You will show {{emet|faithfulness}} to Jacob and loving devotion to Abraham, as You swore to our fathers from the days of old.

mikamokha.gloss: Who is like You? The book closes with a question that is really the highest praise. The prophet's own name, Micah, is a shortened form of Mikayahu, meaning who is like the LORD; so the whole book ends by sounding out his name in worship. The same cry rang at the Red Sea, who is like You among the gods, and here it marvels not at God's power but at His matchless mercy in pardoning sin.

nasa.gloss: To lift, carry, bear away. To pardon iniquity is literally to lift it off and carry it away, the same picture as bearing a burden or removing guilt. It is the language of the scapegoat carrying sin into the wilderness and points forward to the One who bears away the sin of the world, taking our guilt upon Himself and carrying it off for good.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast love, covenant loyalty, loving devotion. Rendered here loving devotion, it names the loyal, merciful love that keeps covenant. God does not retain anger forever because He delights in chesed; His mercy is not reluctant but joyful. This is the same chesed His people were told to love in 6:8, now revealed as the very thing God most delights to show.

emet.gloss: Faithfulness, truth, reliability. God will show faithfulness to Jacob and loving devotion to Abraham, keeping the very oath He swore to the patriarchs centuries before. The final ground of hope is not the people's goodness but God's unbreakable covenant truth, His faithfulness to His own promise, which reaches its yes in Christ.

basic.context: Micah looks around and grieves: it is as though the harvest is over and there is not one good grape or fig left. The godly have vanished, leaders take bribes, and you cannot even trust your closest friend or family; a person's enemies are those in his own house. Yet in that darkness the prophet plants his feet: but as for me, I will look to the LORD, I will wait for the God of my salvation. He owns his sin, trusts God to plead his case, and then the book rises to a breathtaking finish: who is a God like You, who pardons sin and casts it into the depths of the sea, keeping the promise He made to Abraham long ago.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter moves in three steps: honest lament over a corrupt society, a confession of faith in the dark, and a closing hymn of praise to the pardoning God. Verse 6 is quoted by Jesus about the division the gospel brings. The great ending, verses 18 to 20, is not wishful thinking but rests on God's sworn covenant with Abraham, which the New Testament says is kept in Christ. The name Micah itself, who is like the LORD, becomes the last word of praise.
basic.originalLanguages: The climax turns on the prophet's own name. Mi kamokha, who is like You, echoes his name Micah, who is like the LORD. God pardons iniquity, the verb nasa, to lift and carry sin away. He delights in chesed, loyal covenant love, the same word from 6:8. And He shows emet, faithfulness, to Jacob and Abraham, keeping His sworn word.
basic.typology: The God who lifts and carries away sin foreshadows the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. Casting sin into the depths of the sea points to the total, irreversible forgiveness secured at the cross. And the covenant sworn to Abraham is fulfilled in Christ, the seed of Abraham in whom all nations are blessed.
basic.christ: Micah 7:18-20 is the gospel in seed form. God pardons iniquity by lifting it off and carrying it away, and He does this finally in Jesus, who bears our sin in His body on the tree. Our sins are cast into the sea, never to be dredged up, and the promise to Abraham finds its yes in Christ, the offspring in whom the nations are blessed.
basic.god: God is shown as utterly beyond compare, not chiefly in raw power but in mercy: He pardons, passes over transgression, does not keep His anger forever, and delights, actually delights, in showing loyal love. He is faithful to promises made centuries earlier to Abraham. He is a Shepherd who tends His flock, and a Redeemer whose forgiveness is as deep and final as the sea.
basic.crossReferences: Jesus quotes Micah 7:6 in Matthew 10:35-36 about the division He brings. Exodus 15:11 asks who is like You among the gods, the same cry of wonder. Psalm 103:12 pictures sins removed as far as east from west, and Isaiah 53 shows the Servant bearing away our iniquity, fulfilled in Christ, the seed of Abraham of Galatians 3.
basic.oneStory: The book ends by facing the worst, a world without one upright person, and then lifting its eyes to the only hope: the incomparable God who pardons sin, tramples it down, and buries it in the sea. This is the God who keeps faith with Abraham, and the whole promise runs forward to Christ, in whom sin is carried away and every nation is blessed.

context.literary: The chapter falls into a lament (1-6), a psalm of confident trust (7-13), and a closing prayer and doxology (14-20). The opening image of a fruitless harvest sets the tone of grief, then the mood turns sharply at verse 7 with but as for me. The final verses are a hymn built on rhetorical wonder, who is a God like You, stacking verbs of mercy: pardons, passes over, does not retain anger, delights, has compassion, vanquishes, casts into the sea. The book that began with judgment on Samaria ends drowning sin in the deep.
context.historical: Micah ministered in the eighth century, as Assyria devastated the region. The social breakdown he describes, corrupt princes and judges, the collapse of trust even within families, reflects a society in moral freefall. The hope of rebuilt walls and an extended boundary, with people streaming in from Assyria to Egypt, looks past the coming exile to restoration. Bashan and Gilead were rich pasturelands east of the Jordan, symbols of abundance in the days of old.
context.canonical: Micah 7:6 is cited by Jesus in Matthew 10:35-36 and echoed in Luke 12:53. The closing doxology, verses 18 to 20, has shaped Jewish and Christian prayer for centuries and is read in the Tashlich ceremony where sins are symbolically cast into water. Who is a God like You reprises Exodus 15:11, and the appeal to the oath sworn to Abraham ties the book to Genesis 12 and 22, fulfilled according to Galatians 3:16 in Christ, Abraham's seed.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lament honestly the collapse of a godly society, to model steadfast faith in the God of salvation even amid deserved judgment, and to end the book on the incomparable mercy of God who pardons and removes sin and keeps His ancient covenant, so that the remnant hopes not in themselves but in Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The lament of verses 1 to 6 describes a corrupt generation, not a rule to imitate; verse 7, but as for me I will look to the LORD, is the prescribed response of faith in every dark time. The doxology proclaims who God is and what He does, calling the reader to trust and worship the pardoning God rather than merely to admire Him.

debates.question: Is the great forgiveness of 7:18-20 grounded in Israel's repentance or in God's own character and covenant?

views.label: Grounded in God's character and sworn covenant
views.case: The text roots pardon in who God is, He delights in chesed, and in the oath sworn to Abraham and Jacob from the days of old, not in the people's worthiness, which the chapter has just shown to be bankrupt.

views.label: Repentance is the necessary channel
views.case: The confession of verse 9, because I have sinned against Him, shows that pardon is received by those who own their guilt and wait on God, so repentance is the appointed way mercy is embraced.

debates.bearing: Scripture holds both: forgiveness flows from God's free, covenant-keeping character and is received by those who, like the prophet, confess their sin and look to the God of their salvation, a mercy secured once for all in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The lament opens with a harvest metaphor of emptiness, no cluster, no early fig, an image of a land stripped of godliness. The chasid, the godly or covenant-loyal person, has perished. At verse 7 the tone breaks: waani, but as for me, the prophet fixes his hope on the God of his yesha, his salvation. The doxology then gathers the great mercy words. Mi kamokha, who is like You, deliberately sounds the prophet's own name, Mikayahu, who is like the LORD, and reprises the song of Exodus 15:11. God is the one nose avon, who lifts and carries away iniquity, the verb nasa picturing guilt hoisted off and removed. He does not retain his af, his anger, forever, because He delights, chafets, in chesed, loyal covenant love, the same chesed His people were told to love in 6:8. He will vanquish, kavash, tread down our iniquities, and cast, shalak, all our sins into the metsulot, the depths of the sea. And He will keep emet, faithfulness, to Jacob and chesed to Abraham, honoring the oath sworn from the days of old.

typology.type: God who lifts and carries away iniquity (nasa)
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world and bears it in His body, John 1:29 and 1 Peter 2:24.

typology.type: Sins cast into the depths of the sea
typology.fulfillment: The complete, irreversible forgiveness secured at the cross, our sins removed and remembered no more, Hebrews 8:12 and 10:17.

typology.type: The oath of loving devotion sworn to Abraham
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the seed of Abraham, in whom the covenant is kept and all nations are blessed, Galatians 3:16 and Luke 1:72-73.

study.christ: The final three verses of Micah are one of the clearest windows onto the gospel in the prophets. God pardons iniquity by lifting it off and carrying it away, and this reaches its fulfillment in Jesus, of whom John cries, behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. He does not retain His anger forever because He delights in loving devotion, and at the cross that delight in mercy meets His justice, for Christ bears the anger our sin deserved. Our sins are cast into the depths of the sea, buried beyond recovery, so that God remembers them no more. And the whole hope rests on the oath sworn to Abraham, which Paul says is fulfilled in Christ, the offspring of Abraham through whom every nation is blessed. So the book that opened with judgment ends with a Savior in view, and the prophet's own name, who is like the LORD, becomes a hymn to the God who pardons sinners in His Son.
study.god: The last portrait of God in Micah is the deepest: He is incomparable, and what makes Him so is not first His might but His mercy. He pardons iniquity, passes over transgression, refuses to hold His anger forever, and positively delights in loving devotion, His mercy joyful rather than grudging. He has compassion again on a people who deserve none, treads their sins underfoot, and casts them into the sea. And He does all this as the covenant-keeping God who honors an oath sworn to Abraham and Jacob centuries before, proving that the hope of sinners rests not on their fragile faithfulness but on His unshakable emet. This is the God to whom the prophet, and every believer, says, who is a God like You.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes Micah 7:6 about a person's enemies being those of his own household, describing the division the gospel brings.
Who is like You among the gods, the song at the Red Sea that Micah 7:18 reprises as wonder at God's mercy.
As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions, paralleling sins cast into the sea.
The promises were spoken to Abraham and to his seed, who is Christ, fulfilling the oath invoked in Micah 7:20.

study.oneStory: Micah closes the way the whole Bible does, staring honestly at a broken world and then lifting its eyes to the God who saves. Society has collapsed and no one is upright, yet the incomparable God pardons iniquity, tramples it down, and buries every sin in the sea, keeping the covenant love He swore to Abraham. That promise runs straight to Christ, the seed of Abraham, who carries away the sin of the world, so that the prophet's name, who is like the LORD, becomes the church's endless song of grace.

sermon.title: Parenting with Hope in the Worst of Times
sermon.why: Piper preaches Micah 7, moving from the collapse of society and the family in verses 1 to 6 to the steadfast confidence of verse 7, but as for me I will look to the LORD, and on to the God who bears away iniquity and casts our sins into the sea, showing how hope holds even in the darkest times because of God's incomparable, covenant-keeping mercy.

quiz.q: How does Micah describe the society in verses 1 to 6?

options.text: As so corrupt that the godly have vanished and even one's own household cannot be trusted.
options.note: The harvest is empty of good fruit; leaders take bribes and family turns on family.

options.text: As basically healthy with only a few minor problems.
options.note: The picture is total collapse, not minor problems.

options.text: As already destroyed and completely uninhabited.
options.note: People remain, but trust and godliness have collapsed among them.

options.text: As a model community for the nations to imitate.
options.note: It is the opposite: a warning of moral freefall.

quiz.q: What is the turning point in verse 7?

options.text: But as for me, I will look to the LORD and wait for the God of my salvation.
options.note: In the darkness the prophet fixes his hope on God, not on people.

options.text: The prophet decides to trust his friends and family after all.
options.note: He has just said not to trust even them; his hope turns to God alone.

options.text: The enemy finally offers a peace treaty.
options.note: The shift is inward and Godward, not a treaty with enemies.

options.text: The prophet gives up hope entirely.
options.note: This is the opposite of giving up; it is confident waiting on God.

quiz.q: What does it mean that God pardons iniquity in verse 18?

options.text: He lifts our sin off and carries it away, the picture behind the Hebrew verb nasa.
options.note: To pardon is to bear the guilt away, pointing to the Lamb who takes away the world's sin.

options.text: He merely ignores sin as if it did not matter.
options.note: He does not ignore sin; He carries it away, and justice is met in Christ.

options.text: He requires the people to carry their own guilt forever.
options.note: The point is that God Himself lifts and removes the guilt.

options.text: He pardons only those who have never sinned.
options.note: He pardons the guilty remnant, those who have sinned and confess it.

quiz.q: Why is the ending built on the word who is a God like You?

options.text: It sounds out the prophet's own name and marvels at God's matchless mercy in forgiving sin.
options.note: Micah means who is like the LORD; the book ends by turning his name into praise for God's mercy.

options.text: It is a complaint that God is far away and unlike anyone helpful.
options.note: It is wonder and praise, not complaint; God is near and merciful.

options.text: It asks the nations to name their own gods.
options.note: It celebrates the LORD's incomparable mercy, not rival gods.

options.text: It expresses doubt that God can forgive at all.
options.note: It is confident praise that God does pardon and cast sin into the sea.

quiz.q: On what does the final promise of forgiveness ultimately rest?

options.text: On God's own character and the covenant love He swore to Abraham, fulfilled in Christ.
options.note: Hope rests on God's faithfulness to His oath, kept in Christ the seed of Abraham.

options.text: On the people's proven goodness and reliability.
options.note: The chapter has just shown their goodness is bankrupt; hope rests on God.

options.text: On the strength of the rebuilt city walls.
options.note: Rebuilt walls are a sign of restoration, not the ground of forgiveness.

options.text: On the defeat of the surrounding nations by force.
options.note: The ground is God's covenant mercy, not military victory.

questions: Micah grieves that he cannot find one upright person. When the world around you feels morally bankrupt, where are you tempted to put your hope, and how does verse 7 redirect it?
But as for me, I will look to the LORD. What would it look like this week to wait on the God of your salvation instead of on people or circumstances?
The prophet says, though I have fallen, I will arise; though I sit in darkness, the LORD will be my light. Where do you need that confidence right now?
God pardons iniquity by lifting it off and carrying it away, casting our sins into the sea. Do you live as though your sins are truly gone, or do you keep dredging them back up?
God does not merely tolerate mercy, He delights in it. How does knowing that God delights to forgive change the way you come to Him with your sin?

translationNotes.note: A man's enemies are the members of his own household is cited by Jesus in Matthew 10:35-36; see also Luke 12:53, describing the division the gospel can bring within families.

Who is a God like You (mi kamokha) plays on the prophet's own name, Micah, a shortened form of Mikayahu, who is like the LORD. Forms of the Hebrew chesed, rendered loving devotion, range across love, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and covenant loyalty.
You will cast out all our sins into the depths of the sea pictures complete, irreversible forgiveness; the verb for vanquish or subdue our iniquities carries the sense of treading them underfoot.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Micah 7:1-20. All 20 verses reproduced contiguously; poetic lines were joined into running prose per the Makor spine style. Em dashes in the BSB text at verses 10 (covered with shame), 11 (walls will come), and 18 (of His inheritance) were rendered as commas to keep the Makor no-dash style while preserving the wording. Sermon by John Piper on Micah 7, Parenting with Hope in the Worst of Times, verified this session on the Desiring God Micah scripture index.
